title: Macroscopic photon counting beating the Poisson noise limit
authors: Timon Schapeler, Fabian Schlue, Isabell Mischke, Michael Stefszky, Benjamin Brecht, Christine Silberhorn, Tim J. Bartley
abstract: Photon counting is a cornerstone of quantum optics. Here, we demonstrate precisely counting from 0 to over 9000 photons, beating the Poisson noise limit by at least $4.1~\mathrm{dB}$ across this range. We achieve sub-single-photon precision up to 276 photons per pulse. To do so, we multiplex eight intrinsically photon-number-resolving superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors across 128 temporal modes. We use a model-informed characterization of each of the 1024 detection bins, for optimal precision. We perform quantum detector tomography to reconstruct the positive operator valued measures (POVMs) of the complete device, which consists of $1.38\cdot10^8$ matrix elements. At the repetition rate of our experiment of $80~\mathrm{kHz}$, we can precisely count photons corresponding to an optical power of approximately $71~\mathrm{pW}$, bridging the gap from single-photon measurements to high-sensitivity optical power meters. A photon-number-resolving detector of this size, and the tools used to analyze it, will become increasingly important to characterize large quantum states, as well as tasks in precision metrology and optical power standards.
summary: The researchers developed a large-scale multiplexed SNSPD detector capable of counting up to 9000 photons with precision exceeding the standard Poisson noise limit. By combining spatial and temporal multiplexing, they bridged the gap between single-photon detection and classical power metering. This advancement is crucial for characterizing large-scale quantum states and improving precision metrology.
main_problem: The difficulty of building detectors that maintain photon-number resolution at high light intensities and bridging the gap between single-photon detectors and high-sensitivity optical power meters.
main_result: Demonstration of macroscopic photon counting from 0 to over 9000 photons, beating the Poisson noise limit by at least 4.1 dB and achieving sub-single-photon precision up to 276 photons per pulse.
method: A large-scale multiplexed detector using 8 spatial SNSPD bins and 128 temporal modes, combined with model-informed characterization (EMG model) and quantum detector tomography to reconstruct POVMs.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of 8 intrinsically photon-number-resolving superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) integrated with a 128-bin temporal multiplexing network using fiber delay lines.
key_observables: Photon-number distributions, arrival times, reconstructed POVMs, detector efficiency, and the second-order correlation function g2(0).
important_parameters: Optical power of ~71 pW, 80 kHz repetition rate, 1024 total detection bins, and photon numbers scaling up to 15,393 per pulse.
main_assumptions: The use of a zero-inflated model to account for detector blinding; the necessity of temporal pulse broadening to mitigate group-velocity dispersion in the fiber network.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Experimental setup schematic and arrival-time histograms; Fig 2: Workflow for photon-number distribution via LUT; Fig 3: Convolution process and uncertainty vs. photon number plots; Fig 4: Ensemble variance and efficiency vs. photon number; Fig 5: POVM matrix heatmap.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and multiplexing architecture, details the characterization and reconstruction methods (LUT and POVM), presents the performance results regarding noise limits and precision, and discusses systematic effects like detector blinding and efficiency-rate dependence.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and precision metrology as it demonstrates a scalable way to characterize large quantum states and provides a new tool for high-sensitivity optical power standards.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Combination of temporal multiplexing (green box), spatial multiplexing (red box) and intrinsic PNR SNSPDs (orange box). One input pulse is (temporally) split into two pulse trains with 128 sub pulses each, afterwards both pulse trains are (spatially) split onto four SNSPDs, which can resolve the photon number in each of the 8 · 128 = 1024 resulting sub pulses, i.e, bins. The output of each of the eight spatial bins is a stream of 128 arrival times measured by the time tagger, which is depicted in the upper right corner. (b) Arrival-time histogram of the entire pulse train, i.e., all 128 temporal bins for the first spatial bin. The 128 peaks correspond to the individual temporal...

FIG. 2. (a) For every input state that impinges on the detection system, we record up to 1024 arrival times (corresponding to the 8 · 128 = 1024 spatial and temporal bins). For every bin, we have a lookup table (LUT), which is created through an EMG model describing the n = 15 underlying photon-number contributions (b). With this, every arrival time is then translated into a photon-number distribution (c)-(e). (b) The LUT is created by fitting EMG distributions to the arrival-time histograms (see Fig. 1(c)-(e)) for all 1024 bins and then assuming a flat prior distribution. As an example we show the LUT for spatial bin 1 and temporal bin 48. (c)-(e) The photon-number distributions are a...

FIG. 3. (a) From the 1024 individual photon-number distributions generated with the LUT (as depicted in Fig. 2(c)-(e)) we can generate an overall photon-number distribution for every single input state that impinges on the detection system. This is done via a convolution. (b), (c) Photon-number distribution of a single shot for two different incident mean photon numbers (484 and 4904, respectively). These shots are marked in (d) with red and blue dots, respectively. (d) Scatter plot of the measurement uncertainty vs. the measured photon number on a shot-by-shot basis. For every incident mean photon number (corresponding to different colors of the scatter plot dots, see color bar) we plot...

FIG. 4. (a) Ensemble variance vs. ensemble mean from 105 samples for every incident mean photon number, corresponding to the colored cross symbols. These lie on the Poisson noise limit (black dashed line) as we measure coherent light, which follows Poissonian statistics. Above a measured mean of approximately 1000 we see a deviation, which we can attribute to a probabilistic blinding of our detection bins. Our blinding model (orange line) accurately explains the deviation from the Poisson noise limit (see Sec III D in the supplemental document for more detail). (b) The efficiency of the device is the fraction of the measured mean photon number over the incident mean photon number (orange...

FIG. 5. Reconstructed POVM matrix of the entire detector shown as a heatmap of the conditional probabilities. The x axis corresponds to the incident mean photon number and the y axis to the detector outcome, i.e., the measured photon number. The color axis shows the probability of observing the conditional probability of a certain measured photon number given an incident photon number. The top left inset shows a zoom-in of the first 500 outcomes. The bottom right inset shows the content of the main plot on a logarithmic scale for both axes.

FIG. 1. The experimental setup can be divided into four main parts: probe state preparation (blue box), temporal multiplexing (green box), spatial multiplexing (red box) and PNR detection (orange box). A detailed explanation is given in Sec I.

FIG. 2. (a) Outcome matrix PD×M for every second coherent input state in the measured photon number region from 10 to 1000. We include Poisson distributions for every forth coherent input state (for clarity), using the mean of the outcome distribution as the mean of the Poisson distribution, i.e., the distributions are not fitted. This shows, that the ensemble data of 105 samples follows Poissonian statistics from our coherent light source. (b) Outcome matrix for measured photon numbers from 1000 to 1800, to visualize the region where the ensemble mean and variance begin to deviate from the Poisson noise limit (compare Fig. 4(a) in the main document).

FIG. 3. (a), (c) and (e) Oscilloscope traces of the 128 bin pulse train for three different mean photon numbers (low, medium and high, respectively) for a repetition rate of the laser of 80 kHz. Subsequent pulse trains (here differentiated by different colors) are directly aligned after each other. (b), (d) and (f) Oscilloscope traces when the laser repetition rate is 32 kHz. Here, when subsequent pulse trains are separated in time, the ”charging” effect is clearly visible. To avoid this we operate at 80 kHz, to be at a constant DC-offset. The spacing between subsequent traces is approximately 100 ns, i.e., just enough time for the SNSPD to reset after a detection event. The inset in (b)...

FIG. 4. (a) Arrival-time histogram of the last temporal bin (128) for spatial bin 1 for two different incident mean photon numbers per bin. The region of interest corresponds to the slow-axis photons from 0 ps to 450 ps (vertical dashed lines). The fast-axis photons arrive 3.5 ns earlier. The peaks between the two extremes correspond to photons that couple back and forth between the axes at the splice interfaces, due to slight angle mismatches of the polarization-maintaining fibers. (b) Coupling probability pc for all temporal bins for spatial bin 1.
